scenario_title: TTL mismatch on duplicate RR insertion into an existing RRset
test_rationale: This test isolates the underspecified corner where an UPDATE attempts to add an RR that is already present except for TTL. The initial zone provides a single A RRset for ttltest.campus.edu., and the sole UPDATE repeats the same owner/type/rdata with a different TTL. That forces the server to choose between preserving the existing TTL, normalizing the RRset, or rejecting the change. A subsequent QUERY on the same name makes the resulting state observable, while the UPDATE reply itself may also diverge.

group_1.behavior: Accept the UPDATE and preserve the pre-existing RRset TTL, or otherwise normalize to a single TTL without rejecting the update.
group_1.post_state: ttltest.campus.edu. remains present as a single A RRset; follow-up QUERY returns the RRset, typically with the original TTL retained.

group_2.behavior: Reject the UPDATE because the duplicate add carries a conflicting TTL for an existing RR in the RRset.
group_2.post_state: ttltest.campus.edu. remains unchanged with the original A RRset and TTL.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §5.2–5.3 requires RRset TTL consistency, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 does not specify how UPDATE processing should handle an add_rr whose owner/type/rdata already exists with a different TTL. As a result, servers may either accept and normalize/preserve the existing RRset TTL or reject the UPDATE, producing divergent reply codes and potentially different observed TTL handling in the post-state.
